title: Dog Limping on Back Leg After Jumping: Causes and Care Guide
meta_desc: Is your dog limping after a jump? Learn about potential injuries, urgency levels, and how a photo can help our triage team assist your pet.
content_html:
TL;DR: A sudden limp after jumping typically indicates a musculoskeletal injury like a strain or CCL tear; while usually requiring a vet visit within 48 hours, immediate emergency care is necessary if your dog is non-weight bearing or in severe pain.

What causes my dog to limp suddenly after jumping?

When a dog begins limping suddenly after jumping, it typically indicates an acute musculoskeletal injury. This can range from a minor muscle strain or a sprain to more serious conditions like a cranial cruciate ligament (CCL) tear—similar to an ACL injury in humans—or a luxating patella. The impact of landing can also cause minor fractures or soft tissue bruising that makes weight-bearing painful for your pet.

Is it an emergency if my dog starts limping after a jump?

  • The urgency level for a sudden limp after jumping is considered Medium; you should schedule a veterinary appointment within 24 to 48 hours.
  • Seek emergency care immediately if your dog is completely non-weight bearing to rule out fractures or dislocations.
  • Go to the ER right away if your pet has visible swelling or is vocalizing in pain.

How do photos and videos help a veterinarian triage my dog's limp?

  • A video allows us to observe the 'grade' of the lameness and see if the dog is toe-touching or carrying the leg entirely.
  • Clear photos help us identify subtle swelling or an abnormal joint angle.
  • These visual aids allow our team to provide more accurate advice on whether you can wait for a scheduled appointment or if you need to head to the ER right away.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Following a jump, hindlimb lameness in dogs can arise from several causes, including hip luxation (traumatic dislocation of the hip, most frequently a craniodorsal displacement of the femoral head), joint fractures (characterized by lameness, pain, and joint swelling), and palmar carpal breakdown (hyperextension injury leading to collapse of carpal joints). Sacroiliac strain, associated with a history of injury and severe pain in the pelvic or sacroiliac region, can also cause hindlimb lameness. Diagnosis often involves radiography or CT to delineate fractures or luxations. Treatment options include external coaptation, surgical intervention with internal fixation (pins, wires, screws), or arthrodesis. Prognosis for recovery is generally good with appropriate treatment.

Chapter: Orthopedics

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1199)

faqs.question: Can I give my dog over-the-counter human pain relief?
faqs.answer: No. Never give your dog human medications like Ibuprofen, Tylenol, or Aspirin, as these can be highly toxic and even fatal to dogs. Only use medications prescribed by your veterinarian.

faqs.question: Should I keep my dog active to 'walk off' the limp?
faqs.answer: No, you should strictly limit your dog's activity. Rest is crucial. Avoid walks, stairs, and further jumping until a veterinarian has assessed the injury to prevent making it worse.

faqs.question: How do I know if the back leg is broken?
faqs.answer: Signs of a break include a visible deformity, the leg dangling at an odd angle, extreme swelling, or the dog being unable to put any weight on the leg while vocalizing in pain.
